content: Background

Every individual has the right to express their views and place their dissatisfaction in a peaceful manner. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) guarantees equality, individual and life liberty, as well as freedom of thought and expression, and the right to peaceful assembly, among many other rights. Similarly, Nepal signed the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) 1966 and its Optional Protocol on May 14, 1991, and the Second Optional Protocol in March 1998. Thus, the provisions of these covenants and protocols apply obligatorily to Nepal. Section 9 of the Nepal Treaty Act 1990 provides that if treaty provisions conflict with Nepalese law, the Nepalese law shall be ineffective to the extent of the conflict, establishing the primacy of treaties. This covenant plays a vital role in protecting the fundamental rights of citizens of each treaty nation, including freedom of expression and assembly. These sacrosanct values also hold a special place in Nepalese law. Article 17 of the Constitution of Nepal provides for the right to freedom of citizens, protecting the freedom of expression and the freedom to assemble peacefully without arms to manifest such thoughts collectively.

Utilizing these constitutional rights, three separate applications were registered at the District Administration Office, Kathmandu, under the names of Sudan Gurung (from an organization called 'We Nepal'), Ankit Malla, Khemraj Saud, Sabal Gautam, and Purushottam Yadav, requesting to hold a peaceful demonstration in the Maitighar Mandala area on September 8, 2025. Following discussions with the Chief District Officer (CDO) at the District Administration Office, a verbal agreement was reached to demonstrate only in non-prohibited areas and to ensure the demonstration remained peaceful. Accordingly, the District Administration Office, Kathmandu, granted permission. Thus, the plan was to start a peaceful demonstration at Maitighar Mandala on September 8, 2025, and hold a corner meeting at the same location as per the pre-approved schedule.

The demonstration called by the youth on September 8 was targeted against the widespread irregularities in the country. The massive participation of youth in social networks, specific specialized networks, and street movements against poor governance from time to time indicated the youth's anger against existing systemic flaws. Dissatisfaction against these anomalies was also found expressed through comments on social media by ordinary people, articles published in other media/newspapers against corruption, bad governance, and delays in government work, as well as resentment towards the processes of government operation.

Since early September in Nepal, such dissatisfaction was seen pouring out through hashtag campaigns on social networks like TikTok and Reddit, which widely publicized videos and memes accusing the children of politicians of adopting luxurious lifestyles using wealth earned through corruption.

Prior to September 8, Gen-Z movement callers protesting against corruption and social media restrictions had sent posts such as 'Sri Lanka did it, Indonesia did it, Philippines did it, Bangladesh did it, now it's Nepal's turn! It's time for us Gorkhalis to rise. It's time to raise our voice.' AI-generated photos/videos of public structures like the Parliament Building and Singha Durbar being burned or destroyed were also shared on various platforms. Furthermore, news was published in the media that plans for attacks were being made by mapping the houses of top leaders on networks like Netakhor.vercel.app.

There were also various groups on a network named Discord. In one of these Discord groups, views regarding anarchic activities, such as manufacturing Molotov Cocktails, vandalism, looting, and arson, were being shared. One of the organizers, Sudan Gurung, stated before the commission that upon learning of this, they had informed the police on September 7 (Bhadra 22) before the protest and even requested the Cyber Bureau to stop or remove such content.

Social networks have become extremely important and powerful means of communication in today's changing world. This medium has appeared powerful specifically in collectively manifesting the voice of the youth. However, the damage caused through these mediums is equally fast and widespread. There is also a global trend of increasing security threats arising from digital and cyber mediums.
Unawareness of the National Investigation Department

The National Investigation Department had no specific information regarding the messages expressed on these networks. The National Investigation Department appears not to have conducted any analysis of the growing anti-establishment sentiment among the youth. Nor does it appear that any other relevant government agencies conducted any study or research in this regard.

While the three Gen-Z groups that called for the demonstration and received approval from the CDO of Kathmandu stated the protest would be peaceful, it initially started peacefully but began to turn violent after some time. Neither the organizers nor the government security agencies had any inkling that the protest would turn violent in this manner. Police officers assessed that the situation in the capital on September 8 would be peaceful, based on the fact that a National Assembly meeting was scheduled at the Federal Parliament and the President was attending a program at the National City Hall (Rastriya Sabhagriha) that day.

On the evening of September 7 (Bhadra 22), a meeting of the National Security Council was held at the Prime Minister's residence, Baluwatar. During the meeting, heads of all security agencies, including the National Investigation Department and Nepal Police, informed that Gen-Z would demonstrate peacefully on September 8. No information regarding potential violence in the demonstration was provided. According to an official from the National Investigation Department, some intelligence reports did assess that there could be infiltration by some unruly individuals in the peaceful demonstration organized by the youth generation to try and provoke the situation, but there was no analysis that vandalism and arson could occur at the Federal Parliament building.

Instructions were given to remain restrained because youth and students of a specific age group would participate, and to facilitate the peaceful demonstration without using force if possible. According to the then chief of the District Police Range Kathmandu, SSP (now DIG) Bishwo Adhikari, the Inspector General of Police (IGP) Chandra Kuber Khapung had instructed not to use 'heavy deployment' or show too many security personnel since specific age-group youths were involved, but to keep manpower ready in close proximity and use sequential force according to law only if destructive and violent activities like breaking prohibited zones or vandalizing and burning public property occurred.

In the reporting of the National Investigation Department, it was suggested that three thousand to a maximum of five thousand individuals would gather for the demonstration on September 8. All security arrangements were moved forward based on this suggestion, which proved to be insufficient.
Integrated Special Security Plan

Following the announcement of the Gen-Z movement, the Chief District Officer (CDO) of Kathmandu directed the District Security Committee to strengthen security arrangements and coordinate with respective parent agencies for the timely mobilization of additional forces. Based on intelligence from the police administration, intelligence agencies, and district security bodies, the protest was initially perceived as a program to express dissatisfaction regarding social media regulations. Assuming it would remain peaceful in the early stages, the 'Integrated Special Security Plan, 2082' was approved, identifying potential routes, timing, estimated participants, and sensitive areas.

On September 7, 2025 (Bhadra 22, 2082), a meeting of the District Security Committee was held under the chairmanship of the Kathmandu CDO, including high-ranking officials from the Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, Nepal Army, and National Investigation Department. The committee approved the integrated security plan, which included the establishment of command posts, prohibited area cordons, rally escorts, intelligence collection, picketing, and roadblocking. The plan also specified that any manpower shortages would be fulfilled by requesting additional personnel from the relevant security agencies. Consequently, roles were specified under a three-tier security strategy.

content.content: Plan 'A': If the pre-announced program remains peaceful at the designated time and place, security forces will coordinate and collaborate with the organizers to facilitate security and traffic management.

Plan 'B': If there is an attempt to forcibly enter prohibited or sensitive areas, use of sticks or stones, obstruction of government work, vandalism of public or private property, or interference with security personnel, the strategy involves persuasion, informing the mob about legal consequences, and using a graduated application of force to take control.

Plan 'C': In case of fatal attacks on security personnel, armed clashes, forced entry into vital installations, arson, or a situation where casualties are imminent and control requires a curfew, force will be used per the Local Administration Act, 1971. This includes declaring a curfew and requesting Nepal Army mobilization upon a further decision by the District Security Committee.

content: Reflecting on the strategies prepared by government agencies, the meetings held on the evening of September 7 and the morning of September 8 (Bhadra 23) analyzed the peace and security situation in Kathmandu as 'satisfactory' at that time.
Peaceful Demonstration Turns into a Violent Mob

The protest, which began around 9:00 AM on September 8 and was expected to be peaceful, remained normal until 10:15 AM. However, from 11:00 AM onwards, the situation progressively deteriorated. By the time the protesters reached Bijuli Bazar, the gathering had transformed into a massive, unmanageable crowd.

Since it was anticipated that the crowd would only arrive from the Bijuli Bazar side, police barricades were placed only on the main road in front of the Everest Hotel; no barricades were placed on the service lanes. When protesters suddenly entered from all directions through these unguarded routes, the security management became extremely complex.

There was no visible effort to contain the crowd before it merged at Bijuli Bazar. After the failure of Plan 'A', Plan 'B' should have been implemented immediately, but this transition did not occur in real-time. Specifically, as the crowd size increased, the security presence failed to increase proportionately.

By 11:14 AM, when students and individuals from various locations reached the Everest Hotel in New Baneshwor, the protest had taken the form of a massive, agitated mob shouting provocative slogans. Administrative officials have admitted that the security forces present did not have sufficient strength to hold back such a large crowd at that location.
Breaching the Barricades and Entering Prohibited Zones

The crowd behind the front lines at the Everest Hotel began pushing forward while chanting slogans, causing the front-line protesters to crash into and break the police barricades. Protesters coming from the rear (the Bijuli Bazar side) also contributed to toppling the structures. Once the barricades were breached, the police were forced to retreat while deploying water cannons and tear gas, as the protesters pushed forward toward the Federal Parliament building.

Two major weaknesses were noted in the security response near the Everest Hotel. First, the security presence was weak; the deployment should have been increased based on situational assessment. Second, the barricades were placed only on the main road and were of a lightweight, outdated nature. Being only a single line of defense, they were easily bypassed via the service lanes, leaving the police caught in the middle of the crowda significant tactical failure.
Situation Around the Parliament Complex

Around 11:45 AM, as the massive, agitated mob pushed toward the Federal Parliament area while throwing stones, police used water cannons to control the situation. The available water cannon had the capacity to use two jets simultaneously, but doing so exhausts the water in just 3 minutes. CCTV footage shows the water cannon was used twice, but once empty, refilling was not immediately possible. Since the police had only one functional water cannon at the site, it became ineffective after two uses. Although the Armed Police Force had another water cannon at Baneshwor Chowk, it was moved to the South Gate without ever being deployed.

The ineffective use of water cannons further incited the young protesters instead of dispersing them. They began using stones, bricks, and concrete blocks found at the site to attack security forces. Some protesters used slingshots, axes, and other domestic tools, while others shouted incitements to 'capture' the Parliament building. Some even focused their attacks directly on the water cannon vehicle.

Following the failure of the water cannons, security personnel appeared somewhat panicked and began using less-lethal weapons such as batons, tear gas, and rubber bullets. The tear gas proved ineffective as protesters picked up the canisters with wet towels and threw them back at the security forces. As protesters became increasingly aggressive, both Nepal Police and Armed Police Force personnel inside and outside the complex felt they were losing control of the situation.

During this sensitive time, if field commanders had attempted to engage in dialogue with the youth instead of relying solely on force, the situation might have normalized. However, caught between the fear of being blamed for a mob breach and the escalating aggression of the protesters, security forces continued using batons, gas, and rubber bullets. There was a noticeable lack of a 'defusing mechanism' to de-escalate the anger of the young protesters.

As the peaceful demonstration turned into a riot, some organizers, including Sudan Gurung, President of the 'We Nepal' organization, reportedly announced the withdrawal of the movement after failing to calm the crowd. It was evident that the mob had moved far beyond the control of the original protest organizers.
Incitement by Motorcyclists and Escalation of the Mob (September 8)

At approximately 12:00 PM, around 100 motorcyclists arriving from Chabahil, Gaushala, and the Dhobikhola Corridor converged on the Baneshwor area. Some participants wore black T-shirts with 'TOB' (Tibetan Original Blood/The Original Brothers) markings. They actively incited the crowd by revving engines to create loud backfire noises and shouting provocative slogans. This led to a significant escalation, with the mob attacking security forces with stones, iron rods, and slingshots while attempting to breach the West Gate of the Federal Parliament.
The Declaration and Execution of Curfew Orders

The Chief District Officer (CDO) of Kathmandu issued the first curfew order for the Baneshwor area effective from 12:30 PM to 10:00 PM. Subsequent orders covered prohibited zones like Shital Niwas, Baluwatar, and Singha Durbar. The CDO justified these as a 'last resort' under Section 6(A) of the Local Administration Act, 1971. However, communication was a critical failure; police used small handheld megaphones that were drowned out by the massive crowd. Many young protesters, unfamiliar with the concept of a curfew, remained in the area unaware of the legal implications or the impending use of lethal force.
Breach of the Federal Parliament and Use of Force

Protesters successfully breached the Federal Parliament's perimeter, breaking walls in over 400 locations and scaling the South Gate. Arson attacks were launched against security posts and administrative rooms. Radio logs from a female police officer recorded distress calls stating, 'They are going to kill us,' as the mob closed in. Approximately 500 protesters managed to enter the complex before being repelled. Security leadership defended the use of lethal force, citing the 'Doctrine of Necessity' to prevent the total destruction of the Parliament building.
Legal Analysis and Findings of Excessive Force
The Commission's investigation identified several critical legal and procedural violations during the September 8 incident:
1. Premature Use of Force: CCTV footage from Civil Hospital shows injured victims arriving at 12:07 PM23 minutes before the curfew was legally in effect.

2. Indiscriminate Firing: Evidence of live rounds was found in high-rise offices (Hartford Education Center) and local cafes (Himalayan Java, Arabica Café). The shooting was not targeted at the mob's legs but was widespread, hitting individuals inside buildings.

3. Violation of the 'Below the Knee' Rule: Post-mortem reports confirm that most of the 17 immediate fatalities were caused by gunshot wounds to the upper body (chest and head), violating Section 6(1)(B) of the Local Administration Act.

4. Ballistic Accountability: There is a complete lack of records regarding weapon assignments and ammunition expenditure. Both the Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force (APF) failed to provide ballistic audit logs, citing the destruction of records during the subsequent day's arson.
Ammunition Expenditure Summary (September 8)
Reports indicate the following rounds were fired by security forces in the Kathmandu Valley on the first day of the uprising:

* Lethal (Metal) Bullets: 52 Rounds
* Rubber Bullets: 417 Rounds
* Blank Rounds: 117 Rounds
* Tear Gas Shells: 1,017 Rounds (Many were found to be expired and ineffective)
Failure to Preserve Evidence in the Baneshwor Incident (September 8)

During the clashes on September 8 (Bhadra 23), security forces utilized lethal weapons. Standard operational procedure requires the immediate securing of ballistic evidence, such as spent shell casings. However, no such record was maintained. Several factors contributed to this failure: the high-tension environment that persisted until evening, the prioritization of 'situation control' over forensic collection, and the potential contamination of the site by journalists, protesters, and pedestrians.
